speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three, interview with Chaim Engel. When you were helping to kill the German officer, did it make you feel in any way that you were getting even? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how to explain the feelings? The-- the act on killing somebody is terrible, no matter what, even for revenge-- you know for-- I think what you have to-- saving your own life-- things that I do-- make a terrible feeling. But I-- there was no way the other way. There was not other way. It was either him or me. Though--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said this is for my brother. This is for my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I said it was-- brother, my father, and all the Jews who you killed. I still even don't like [INAUDIBLE]. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got to the forest, the woods, you met these other-- this other group.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met the other people, and I was a little relieved. I thought, at least here more people. We maybe can stay together, and whatever happened. But they didn't accept us, because some of us not speaking Polish, not Yiddish. And they didn't accept someone. So he jumped with a gun-- with a revolver-- of a-- rifle. And he want to shoot me. And someone jumped in the front of me. And I guess that kept the man from doing. So I decided, forget about. We don't go with them. We go on our own.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to Selma, forget about. We go. And I tried to find the farmer what I worked before I came to Sobibor. And I believe he was nice to me-- not that he might help you or not. But at least there's some connection. So I thought maybe do. So we went on the stars. We would look from the stars of the-- the-- how you call it-- the big fang? 'Beh'-- The Milky Way? But not the Milky Way. The--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The fire-- like a wagon. I forgot what was English word for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn to read the stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [LAUGHS] You were born there. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I knew about that. So-- The Big Dipper.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it-- the Big Dipper. So we looked at the Big Dipper. I didn't know if I went the right direction. Right away, I had something to go by.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we went-- now, the first night, I'm not yet so far. Yeah-- so we-- we-- separated from this group. And-- and we had to go somewhere during the day-- be there. So we went. We walked. We walked the whole night. In the morning, we were five kilometers from camp. And we went into the first house there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took out some valuables-- I think money, dollars, or whatever. And I said to the man, just let me stay over during the day. And you-- that can you have. You don't have to give much of anything, just a piece of bread or something. And the morning, we go. And he agreed. He kept us. And in the same village, the Germans-- because the Germans start to look all over them. From the army, they came. And they sent out special groups to look for all this-- what run away. So in the same village where we were heading, they picked a whole bunch of them into a charter. So luck was really with us. Excuse me. They didn't caught us. And he didn't tell-- tell anything. So-- so everything was-- was OK. And in the morning-- oh, in the evening-- we left. And we left. And we went to the woods. And we did it for-- I don't know-- about a week, two weeks, something like that. We had a hard time. Sometimes, we find somebody to keep us. Sometimes, we were in the woods, just in the woods like that. And sometimes, we were-- we didn't have the woods even, because we went sometimes in a hoy--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hay.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Haystacks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Haystacks. We were hidden in haystacks. And one time, we were hidden in haystacks, and some kids climbed up and they saw Jews. And they said, Jews! Jews! Things like that. So-- so we had to go away from there in the middle of the day. And then, when we-- when we woke, there came two wagons and-- farmers and wagon. And yeah, they will ask, give us a ride. And I hear what they say in Polish. They didn't know I understand Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you speak to them in? In German?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't speak to the-- to who?

speaker: interviewer
text: To the drivers.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I didn't. I-- they just offered us a ride.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went on without saying anything. And they didn't know I speak Polish. And I hear one to us says, oh. Let them take him and do whatever that is. So I said to Selma, jump! And she jumped. And I jumped. And we let them go. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you speak to Selma?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe-- I spoke German. And she understands German. And that maybe was the mix of German, Jewish, things like that. So that was a-- but later, I-- I learned Dutch. Yeah. But mainly we spoke-- I guess in German or Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and to come back to the story, we-- we walked for-- we had an experience, about a week or two. But every time I went into somewhere, I said, listen. If you want to keep us over the war, we have valuables, and we give you everything we have. And we don't want much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And because Selma is a girl, you know, a young girl, he kept us maybe. And he says, he says, well, I cannot keep you, because there's too much houses around. But I have a brother that lives not far. And he is more isolated. He said, he might be willing to keep you for a while. So, OK. So he dressed Selma up as an old lady. And me, he put off in the wagon covered with branches. And he drove us about 10 kilometers. If they would have caught us, the Germans, on the road, we all would have been dead, even the farmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you trust him?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't have any choice. I trust him. I went in-- I didn't have any choice. I mean, that's the only way I could-- the only thing I could try. It's the only thing. If I-- if not, not. But at the least this maybe something-- some hope. And he brought us to his brother. And he talked with his brother. And the brother agreed. And he kept us. He kept us for nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he keep you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in-- he kept us-- the cows were-- the barn. The cows were downstairs. And upstairs he made straw. Put on the-- they keep always straw there. And he kept us there, under the things. So he brought us-- once a day, he brought us some food. And I made the barrel for the [INAUDIBLE]. It was there. And he come one day-- every day he came to take it away. And-- never went really to take a shower-- once in a month, maybe-- things like that. And one day, this wife probably, she thought that maybe we didn't give him everything we had. So they made us sleep in by the pigs. And they checked everything there. But we really give. We were honest. We gave them everything we had. So I had one gold pin, I had. And Selma had this 'scappies', or whatever they call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Scabies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Scabies, or something. And you can buy only with-- how do you call phosphor-- when you make the matches in them-- how [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Phosphorus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Phosphorus-- something-- some kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ointment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some ointment. They can use it, and that helps. So I want them to go and buy that. So I gave them the gold pen. I said, take that and bring some ointment. So he went. And that helped a little bit. So-- so there was something.

speaker: interviewer
text: The two of you were all alone in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We always were alone. And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you move? Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As I say, no, really. The only thing, when they took us, that we can take some washing ourselves once a month or whatever. Then we moved down. But otherwise, we didn't move. And-- what was I going to say? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I sometimes snored. And-- and he hear that. He says, be careful, because they can hear it, and things like that. So Selma always waked me when I was snoring. And one day, we had-- we cut through the roof. We could see there was some place where storks were there. And one day, the storks are gone. So he cames and tells us, he says, you have to go. If storks go away from a place, that is not a good sign.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we talked him over. We told him, that's OK. They will come back. And some of the storks came back. And it was OK. So no matter how they treated us, they saved our life. Because how many people did what they did? I hear later friends that did the same thing-- at night, they killed them and took the money without keeping them. So we were really lucky. So-- When you think back on those months, what do you remember as being-- what do you-- what do you remember most about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: About being hidden. What was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I still have the feeling that they did a big thing for us. I forgot all this negative things about. It was one big positive thing. That's what I remember, you see. No matter how he treats us, he treated us better than every other-- all the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I really have a good feeling about. And we-- we-- Selma probably told you that-- that later when we were freed, and we told the Jewish partisans what happened to us, and we left the money. So they went to him. And he took all the money away from him. What we feel very bad about it. That was not our intention. We didn't get anything of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so we-- we sent them since-- we sent them all the years, we send them, every year money, three, four times a year, we send money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is their name? What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adams. The both brothers died. And the wife-- the wife lives with the son-- not in the same place where we were hidden. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names are-- what's their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Novack. We-- they got the recognition about it, because we send it in. There was some [INAUDIBLE] recognition for that. They had to go especially [INAUDIBLE] they get the recognition. Not to get any money from them, but we send them money. If we go speaking somewhere, if they ask us for donation, so we say send it to-- we let them send it to these people-- money, because we don't take anything. So-- so we send them [INAUDIBLE] for Easter-- oh, for Christmas. So we'll just send them again money. We always do it. We do both families. Although, the other brother didn't do much. But we still feel obligated. To the one we send a little more than the other. But we always send them money. When we left, when the-- the freedom came by the Russians. One day-- this was really luck. A day before the Russians came, his children of-- some kids came there. And somewhere they were playing. And a bird flew and over. We were resting. We were sitting there. And the kids ran up there to get the bird. And they saw us. And you know, that was a big secret no one knew about. So there was really a danger. But luckily, the next day, the Russians came. And we heard shooting, the guns, and things like that. So the Russians came, and they freed us. So We went down. And we went down just for the first time. And the Russians were pretty nice to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your health like at that point? What was your health like at that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess, all right-- weak, but all right. We were young at the time. So-- it's all right. We got-- we could live on it. We didn't move too much. We-- although they gave us once a day food. But I think we really were not especially hungry and things like that. But we survived. And-- and then, he brought us-- he gave us 10 zlotys or whatever it is-- 10 zloty. And then we went to Lublin. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --I don't remember exactly how it went. Anyway, so we had to make a legal living. And I don't know how I got some money. And I went and I bought things. And I went to the people later. When-- when the Germans move away, there was some more things we could buy. So I went there, and I bought things. And I brought it back. And it was a terrible time, a dangerous time, because there was not transportation. The way I traveled is I hitchhiked by train. The freight wagons-- I went on a train, the train went in that direction. I went there and on the open flat. And I brought merchandise back and sold it. And then-- and Selma was about six weeks, sometimes, she was alone. And we had the child there. And because we lived a year in Lodz-- in Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the baby was born in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Parczew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you leave Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin we left, because-- because that is when we went already to Holland. We left-- you see, Lublin was the biggest city. And the biggest city was still some opportunity to make some money, to make a living. But I did different things before then. Because whenever we came out, they want to take-- they want to take me in the army. And I don't feel like I will do-- went through to go to the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I made false papers. And I was 10 years older. I had a mustache and things like that. I did look older. And I was working in a hospital as a nurse. And I did that for a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had enough of it, because I had to see amputated legs, and hands, and things like that. And I-- blood is not something I like to see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't-- then, I started to buy and sell things like that. Not in big scale, because we didn't have much. I don't know how I had the money to buy the first thing even. But anyway, somehow we had it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sometimes we bought from this Russian soldier. In Lublin, I bought sometimes from the Russian soldiers sugar, and I was very quick to sell to these things. So there was quick change-- make some money. And that's the way we make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Polish people treat the Jews who had come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were anti-Semites all the time-- all the time. And all of them, they're terrible. They're-- they're not-- when we were running around to get somewhere, even when we open the door, asked for water, we couldn't get that. They'd say-- they waited already for-- that what happen. They-- they stole the houses from the Jews-- everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the people that went in in the houses from the Jews-- and the Friedlander-- it was one of the organizers of the camp-- he made it outside alive. And when he came back, he came back for a house on his parents. So they killed him, not to give him the house. There were things like that that happened very often. So when people, Jews, went back, they couldn't-- they-- they tried to kill them and not to give them back. So there was-- the treatment was terrible. Did you ever think about staying in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no. No. Never. Never. I even don't want to go back there. My whole family got killed there-- my whole family. The only thing that I have left is a cousin here in Portland who is also a survivor of the war. He is-- my mother and his father were brother and sister. And my father and his mother were brothers and sisters. So we're double--

speaker: interviewer
text: Double first cousins.

speaker: interviewee
text: Double first cousins. Yeah. That is my only, really, family. And I have some second cousins in Israel and here in Los Angeles. But that is really my whole family. I have a cousin in England. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you and Selma leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some talk about that there is a transport of Dutch soldiers that they can go back to Holland. The war was still going on, because that was in 19-- when we were freed, was the war was still going on. Because the Russians stopped next to Warsaw, next to-- [INAUDIBLE]. And they didn't go further for six months. That had happened this time. And right there, they-- they came. They want to bring all the Dutch soldiers, which were in Poland for-- somehow were prisoners or whatever-- to bring them back to Holland. And we hear about it. And I pretended I am Dutch. I didn't talk, because my Dutch-- I didn't have any Dutch living here. And so-- and with the child together-- and she talked with some Dutch Captain. And she told them, I think, the situation. And he agreed to keep us. He took us on the boat. Now, we were the only-- I was the only one not Dutch that was on this boat. Because there was some other people who tried to get in, but couldn't get in there. So-- and our child died on the boat. And they had-- I don't know-- the food or whatever, food poisoning or something like that. And they was buried in the roof-- in-- in the-- an ocean by the Greek-- by Greece. And then, we came to-- to-- Marseilles. And in Marseilles, there-- there was-- they gave us through un-lousing, they called it-- folded everything, changing everything, because they were afraid of bringing so many sicknesses. And then, by train-- oh-- before that happened, we were three months in Odessa. When we came from Lublin, we traveled by train to Odessa. Now, it was very slow. It was three months it took us. We went through Bucharest, Romania, things like that, until we came in Odessa. And in Odessa, they had a special camp for us. And there we were also about three months. As a matter of fact, the father of Frank-- Anna Frank-- he was not yet famous in that time-- he was there too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So over there it was not so bad, because the Red Cross supplied some food for us. So and then, we went by boat to Marseilles. And from Marseilles you went by train.

speaker: interviewee
text: By train to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you think about Holland when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always had a terrific-- I believe in Holland she told me always about-- so I didn't-- I was really very happy about Holland, because it was so different-- day and night with Poland and Holland. They-- but Selma did not tell me-- because when we came back, she said, the more people back, things like that, instead to welcome you. So she didn't immediately had a bad feeling about that. And-- but for me, I'll talk about-- first of all, we came to her mother's hotel, mother/father's hotel. Also, her brother was there. But we had a home. We got-- we got in something. It was not ideal. But anyway, it was something come in. And I-- I-- adjusted to the new thing. Well, it was not so hard for me, because it was really our main business. But-- so I worked there too. And we worked very hard. And then, she didn't-- we didn't work together. We were hard workers-- and my-- her brother and sister-in-law, they were more lazier people. They-- they think it goes by itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got enough of it. And I wanted to get out. Now, he gave me a choice. You see, you have to have a license to have this kind of hotel. And he said, you want it, you can have it. So I said, no, that is not right. I'm a stranger here. And he is the son of the family, and I think he should have it. So we gave the-- him, that he has the hotel. And we started a textile business with a partner, what was our own thing to do. Because it was not the right-- probably I should have listened to Selma. She had said this is not the right person.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, so he had the location. And I had-- I could get from her family's family-- her family-- from her family-- also a license. Because in Holland, whatever you do, you need a special license for it. So if-- you cannot just start a business just like that. You have a special license. So I could get this kind of a license to start the business. And we had some money. We came out on the hotel. So-- and he had the location. So we figure we can make one thing. And for years, it was fine. We did make very good. We made a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like life in Holland then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved it in Holland. I loved it in Holland. But it was-- well, for me it wasn't not that I left that all my family is killed. And I didn't have any feeling that I have anti-Semites in [INAUDIBLE]. Although they were there. But I didn't know that. I didn't know that. So-- so for me, life was now-- and then, we-- there was something in the business didn't go right. And then-- I also-- I have to leave the business. And I was starting other business-- I was not so successful with. And I decided-- and I wanted-- I said let's go. I wanted to go to America. But you couldn't get-- the quota was very minimal for Polish quota. So we could go to Israel. So I said, all right. Let's go to Israel. So we went to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first day I came there, I already thought, how do I go away from here? I couldn't adjust to the life. And we were at that time, we-- we went to a place where a [NON-ENGLISH] I don't know if you know what it is. [NON-ENGLISH] is you work together, but you live on your own.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what sounds good-- and they told us you have to take so many things with you. We took a Frigidaire and an icebox. I don't know. We took everything with us. And we bought-- we bought-- they told us where we-- where we went to there was big mud, a lot of mud. So you need a rubber-- shoes. And because the kids were at the age of growing, so we--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were your children then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. That was in '51. They were born 40-- they were born '46 and '48-- so 5, 6, or 7 years, something. They were very young. So I bought-- I don't know for many-- but maybe I had 20 pair of these rubber boots, because different ages, and for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we came on the boat in Israel, he said, why don't you go begin a business. And-- [LAUGHS] so I explained to him what it is. So I mean, I'm trying to say we took everything with us. And we lived there, but I didn't like it. What didn't you like about it right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in business all these years. And I came there, and everybody tell me you go tomorrow here, work in there. You do that. I worked in the open field. The sun was like an oven. And I was sitting and sweating there. And I didn't want that. I didn't want that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started to go outside, find whatever else to do. We didn't take any money with us. As I said, we took with us things. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --that means we have to stop again-- like before I start the other thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We can stop right here.
